coredata.dc:title: 3D-printing and MATLAB assisted ratiometric-sensing platform for visual on-site sensing of tetracycline using color conversion strategy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruimei
Zeng, Dong
Guo, Yuxin
Huang, Jiawei
Wen, Ruizhi
Li, Hongchang
coredata.dc:description: In this research, a ratiometric-sensing platform (ZIF-8/CDs) that combines non-fluorescence metal–organic frameworks (ZIF-8) with blue-green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been developed for the determination of tetracycline (TC) with high sensitivity and selectivity in real samples. For this purpose, ZIF-8 with polyhedral structure was synthesized, which could form aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and coordination interactions in the presence of TC, leading to enhanced intensity with a bright yellow fluorescence at 516 nm, acting as a response signal. However, the fluorescence intensity of blue-green fluorescent CDs maintained practically constant at 466 nm, serving as a reference signal. Furthermore, we integrate MATLAB algorithm and 3D-printing technology with the ZIF-8/CDs complex into a ratiometric-sensing platform for semi-quantitative detection of TC, in which 3D-printing technology is responsible for engineering an integrated dark chamber to achieve the fluorescence images of real samples, whereas MATLAB with the image processing functions and data processing capabilities utilizes to develop a mini program for calculating the RGB values of the obtained images. Consequently, the ratiometric-sensing platform in a simple, rapid, and economic way without any complicated process and expensive instruments could be used for visual/onsite detection of TC in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D-printing
MATLAB algorithm
Ratiometric-sensing platform
Visualization
Semi-quantitative
Tetracycline detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of ratiometric-sensing platform and its application for sensing TC
Visual detection of TC using the as-proposed strategy
Characterization of CDs, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs complex
Real samples assay using fluorescence spectrophotometry
Visual detection using 3D-printing and MATLAB algorithm assisted RGB mode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the as-synthesized ratiometric fluorescent probe based on ZIF-8/CDs complex for the detection of TC.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs, insets showed the corresponding photographs under UV light with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, respectively. (C) HRTEM image of CDs, insets showed the corresponding particle size distribution diagram of CDs; (D-E) The HRTEM images of ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs, insets showed the corresponding HRTEM images of ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs under different magnification. (F) XRD spectra of ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs. (G) XPS survey spectra of ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs. (H-I) The corresponding high-resolution spectra of C 1 s, and Zn 2p for ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of ZIF-8/CDs, ZIF-8/TC and ZIF-8/CDs/TC. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the reactions of Zn2+, 2-Meim and ZIF-8 before and after the addition of TC. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the reactions of Zn2+, 2-Meim and ZIF-8 before and after the addition of TC. (D) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8, ZIF-8/CDs, ZIF-8/CDs/TC. (E) XPS survey spectra of ZIF-8/CDs and ZIF-8/CDs/TC. (F-G) The corresponding high-resolution spectra of C 1 s and Zn 2p. (H) XRD spectra of ZIF-8/CDs and ZIF-8/CDs/TC. (I) Zeta potential measurements of ZIF-8/CDs and ZIF-8/CDs/TC.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ZIF-8/CDs complex with various concentrations of TC (0.03–60 μM). (B) CIE chromaticity diagram of ZIF-8/CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TC, insets showed the corresponding photographs under UV light with excitation wavelength of 365 nm, respectively. (C) the corresponding linear relationship between ratio intensity (F516/F466) and TC concentration. (D) Repeatability of fluorescence probe for the detection of TC. The error bars represented the standard deviation of three independent measurements.
(A) The 3D-printing device designed by the SHAPR3D program; (B) The visual detection using 3D-printing and MATLAB algorithm assisted RGB mode.
Detection of TC in real samples using the as-proposed ZIF-8/CDs complex.

head.ce:title: 3D-printing and MATLAB assisted ratiometric-sensing platform for visual on-site sensing of tetracycline using color conversion strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Changsha 410005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Changsha 410005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Certification & Inspection Group Hunan Co., Ltd, Changsha 410021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Certification & Inspection Group Hunan Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Certification & Inspection Group Hunan Co., Ltd, Changsha 410021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, a ratiometric-sensing platform (ZIF-8/CDs) that combines non-fluorescence metal–organic frameworks (ZIF-8) with blue-green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been developed for the determination of tetracycline (TC) with high sensitivity and selectivity in real samples. For this purpose, ZIF-8 with polyhedral structure was synthesized, which could form aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and coordination interactions in the presence of TC, leading to enhanced intensity with a bright yellow fluorescence at 516 nm, acting as a response signal. However, the fluorescence intensity of blue-green fluorescent CDs maintained practically constant at 466 nm, serving as a reference signal. Furthermore, we integrate MATLAB algorithm and 3D-printing technology with the ZIF-8/CDs complex into a ratiometric-sensing platform for semi-quantitative detection of TC, in which 3D-printing technology is responsible for engineering an integrated dark chamber to achieve the fluorescence images of real samples, whereas MATLAB with the image processing functions and data processing capabilities utilizes to develop a mini program for calculating the RGB values of the obtained images. Consequently, the ratiometric-sensing platform in a simple, rapid, and economic way without any complicated process and expensive instruments could be used for visual/onsite detection of TC in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is one of the most commonly used groups of antibiotics, which is used as a veterinary medicine and growth promoter in cattle, poultry, and aquaculture, as well as in human therapy [1,2] The presence of TC in the environment may have a critical impact on the development and dissemination of resistant microbes and resistance genes, thereby accelerating the emergence and spread of antibiotic resistance. These resistant microbes can enter the human body through direct or indirect contact and confer resistance to humans, posing a threat to public health [3,4]. Due to the low concentrations of TC in the environment and the complexity of the environmental matrix, precise quantification of TC in the environment requires the development of a comprehensive set of sample pretreatment methods and instrumental detection techniques. So far, chromatographic techniques have been widely used for separating and analyzing compounds in complex environmental samples. Gas chromatography (GC) is typically effective in detecting substances with high volatility. However, compounds with low volatility, such as TC, require derivatization to increase their volatility before they can be detected by GC [5]. This process is complex, time-consuming, and requires a lot of labor. With the advancement of chromatographic techniques, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) has emerged as the preferred method for detecting TC due to its high sensitivity and low LOD values, which can reach as low as μg/L level [6,7]. In addition to chromatographic techniques, mass spectrometry (MS) and immunoassay techniques have also been applied for the detection of TC [8,9]. However, these measurements have high requirements, and need expensive and sophisticated instruments, professional personnel, and time-consuming sample processing. As a result, their practical applications are severely limited. With the high-speed development of instrument techniques, fluorescence-based assays have gained significant attention owing to their low cost, simplicity, rapid response, high sensitivity, and ease of visualization for targets [10–12]. However, single-channel fluorescence-based sensors often suffer from low reliability and selectivity, because they are susceptible to interference from environmental and instrumental factors. A dual/multi-channel fluorescence strategy with a built-in self-calibration function not only improves the sensitivity of detection but also gains the dynamic detection range, which is considered to be the preferred method for the semi-quantitative assay using color conversion strategy in resource-poor areas [13].

MATLAB is a software platform for numerical computing, data analysis, and visualization. It provides a variety of tools and functions for processing and displaying 3D image data, such as volume rendering, image segmentation, and image registration [14]. MATLAB also supports interfacing with 3D printers, which can create physical models of 3D objects from digital data. 3D printing as an emerging technology can be used for visualizing analysis, such as the distribution of chemical elements, the morphology of biological structures, or the construction of portable devices [15]. Hence, MATLAB algorithm and 3D-printing technology can work together to enhance the understanding and communication of ratiometric-sensing platform.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterials with remarkable optical and physicochemical properties [16]. CDs have many advantages, such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost, biocompatibility, and abundant sources [3]. CDs can be synthesized by various methods, such as top-down and bottom-up approaches, surface passivation, and heteroatom doping [17]. CDs have wide applications in environmental or food analysis, such as sensing, encryption, catalysis, and biomedicine [18]. CDs can detect various analytes, encrypt valuable information, enhance photocatalytic reactions, and image biological events. CDs are promising nanomaterials for the development of new optical, energy, and biomedical technologies [19].

ZIF-8 is a metal-organic framework (MOF) composed of zinc ions and 2-methylimidazole ligands. It has a highly porous structure and excellent chemical and thermal stability under various reaction conditions [21]. ZIF-8 has attracted much attention for its applications in gas adsorption, separation, catalysis, and sensing [22]. In particular, ZIF-8 exhibits remarkable aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties, which make it a potential luminescent material for environmental or food analysis [23]. The AIE phenomenon of ZIF-8 is attributed to the restriction of intramolecular rotation of the imidazole ligands upon aggregation, which suppresses the non-radiative decay pathways [24]. ZIF-8 can interact with various AIE molecules, e.g., TC, to form hybrid materials with enhanced fluorescence and tunable emission colors [25]. ZIF-8 can also be used as a host for encapsulating AIE molecules, which can improve their stability and solubility [22]. ZIF-8 is a promising MOF material for the development of novel luminescent sensors and devices.

Inspired by the situations mentioned above, as illustrated in Fig. 1 , ZIF-8 integrated with CDs through electrostatic interaction could establish an efficient and novel ratiometric-sensing platform ZIF-8/CDs complex for the determination of TC concentrations. In this research, the blue-emitting CDs located at 466 nm could not react with TC, serving as the reference signal, while greenish-yellow-emitting TC centered at 516 nm owing to the AIE mechanism between TC and ZIF-8, regarding as the response signal. Accordingly, the as-prepared ratiometric probe ZIF-8/CDs complex was deservedly applied for the detection of TC. Additionally, with the aid of MATLAB algorithm and 3D-printing technology, the simple mini-program and portable detection device were carried out for on-site/visual detection of TC in real samples with semi-quantitative RGB mode, offering a very broad application prospect.

All the chemicals and solvents were at least analytical grade and commercially available. Dimethylimidazole (C4H6N2), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7⋅H2O), and ethylenediamine (C6H8N2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd（Shanghai, China）. Tetracycline hydrochloride (C22H25ClN2O8) was purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The ultra-pure water (resistivity greater than 18.0 MΩ cm−1) utilized for the whole experimental process was purified thoroughly through the Milli-Q system.

The absorption spectra were recorded by UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Aoyi Instruments, Shanghai). The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), where the emission spectra were carried out using 10 nm/10 nm slit widths. The size and morphology of ZIF-8, CDs and ZIF-8/CDs were conducted using high-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM) operating at an acceleration voltage of 300 KV (FEI Talos 200S, USA). X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were collected by an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance X, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS20 Fourier Transform Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The zeta potential of ZIF-8/CDs and ZIF-8/CDs/TC composite were studied using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern, UK). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by an XPS spectrometer using an Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV) with an operating power of 150 W (Thermo Scientific, America).

The synthesis of CDs with a minor modification was carried out according to the previous report [26]. Firstly, 0.4200 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved with 10 mL of ultra-pure water and the resulting solution was sonicated for 5 min to ensure even dissolution. Subsequently, 200 μL of ethylenediamine solution was added to it to form the mixture, and then transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor with a stainless-steel jacket. The reaction was kept at 200 °C for reacting 4 h. The as-prepared product was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was then dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da in the dark. Finally, 5 μL of stock solution of the purified CDs was taken and diluted with 8 mL of ultrapure water for further use.

Under the previous report [27], for preparing the ZIF-8 MOFs, 0.1450 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate and 0.3250 g of dimethylimidazole were dissolved with 10 mL of ultra-pure water in a centrifuge tube, and stirred the mixture at 10000 rpm for 40 min at room temperature, then centrifuged the obtained ZIF-8 solution at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the supernatant. After washing the as-synthesized ZIF-8 three times, it could be dried under an oven at 60 °C for 12 h, and finally stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric-sensing platform and its application for sensing TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to sense TC, the ZIF-8/CDs complex was prepared by simply mixing ZIF-8 with CDs. Typically, the mixture of CDs (1 mL) and ZIF-8 aqueous solution (1 mL, 600 μM) was adequately diluted to 10 mL in a centrifuge tube with Tris-HCl buffer (0.2 M, pH=8.0). After sonication for 15 min, the ZIF-8/CDs complex was prepared as a stock solution. The as-prepared sensing aqueous solution (400 μL) was fully incubated with 100 μL of different concentrations (0–60 μM) of TC standard solution for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence changes in the ratio of ZIF-8/CDs complex (F516/F466, excited with 380 nm) toward TC were determined using an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The specificity and anti-interference evaluation were further investigated through the following steps: initially, the as-prepared solution containing ZIF-8/CDs complex (400 μL) was added to a centrifuge tube with various substances (400 μL, 500 μM), such as cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+), anions (Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−), antibiotics (AZM, CIP, OFX, PEN), and amino acids (AA, Cys, Lys, Glu). Subsequently, this reaction mixture was incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Finally, the ratio changes of ZIF-8/CDs composite (F516/F466, excited with 380 nm) against TC were measured using the F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of TC using the as-proposed strategy
ce:section.ce:para: In order to establish the intelligent ratiometric-sensing platform, a three-dimensional model was designed through the SHAPR3D program and printed successfully using a 3D printer. The device includes a lamp holder with an ultra-violet (UV) excitation light source, a sample slot, two filters (365 nm/400–640 nm), and a dark chamber. Finally, the four parts are combined with the smartphone to build a sensing platform based on smartphone imaging. Milk, honey, and tap water were chosen as the actual samples. The ratiometric fluorescent probe ZIF-8/CDs composite was incubated with different concentrations of TC, and then Tris-HCl buffer solution was supplemented to a total volume of 1 mL in a centrifuge tube. After reacting at room temperature for 5 min, the solution was transferred to a colorimetric cuvette and placed into the as-proposed 3D printed sample pool as the ratiometric-sensing platform. The fluorescence color of the solution was captured by the smartphone camera and transmitted to MATLAB software, in which a designed mini program could convert the image information into computer-processable digital signals (i.e., RGB values), and then analyze the average values of red (R), green (G), and blue (B) in the same rectangular area of the collected image. Therefore, a good linear relationship could be carried out between the TC concentrations and the obtained RGB values (such as R/B, R/G) for the semi-quantitative detection of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs complex
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, and ZIF-8/CDs were presented in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2A, the CDs with blue-green fluorescent exhibited a remarkable absorption peak at 370 nm, which could be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C-N and C-O bond of the CDs [26]. Whereas, ZIF-8 had no obvious absorption peak in the range of 300–800 nm. Concerning the ZIF-8/CDs composite, the absorption spectrum was a combination of the absorption peaks of CDs and ZIF-8, where no any new peak was formed, implying that the ratiometric-sensing platform integrated ZIF-8 with CDs was constructed by simply mixing. Fig. 2B showed that the CDs had a strong emission centered at 466 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. To verify that the as-synthesized material could be used in further applications, the stability of the material should be investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained basically the same, indicating that they had excellent stability. In Fig. 2B, there was no fluorescence emission observed from ZIF-8 alone, when combined with CDs, the emission peak of the ZIF-8/CDs complex at 466 nm was the same compared with that of the CDs. This indicated that the ZIF-8 did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and the ZIF-8/CDs composite could be used as a proportional probe [26]. Furthermore, the ZIF-8/CDs composite also exhibited excellent fluorescence stability (as described in Fig. S2).

Moreover, the morphology of ZIF-8 before and after the encapsulation of CDs was investigated by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 2C, the as-prepared CDs exhibited well mono-dispersity and mostly uniform spherical dots in aqueous solution with an average particle size of 2.52 nm. While the HRTEM images of ZIF-8 were displayed in Fig. 2D, in which the morphology of ZIF-8 was the polyhedral structure with uniform and well-dispersity. It could be seen from Fig. 2E that the shape and size of ZIF-8/CDs were essentially the same as those of ZIF-8, indicating that the incorporation of CDs did not affect the skeleton of ZIF-8.

Moreover, we used XRD spectra to study the crystal structures of ZIF-8 and ZIF-8/CDs in Fig. 2F, we could conclude that the structure of the as-synthesized ZIF-8 was consistent with that of the simulated XRD standard card, and the XRD patterns of ZIF-8/CDs matched with ZIF-8, confirming that the crystal structure of ZIF-8 was not damaged after the embedding of CDs [27]. Subsequently, The XPS spectra were also employed to validate the oxidate state and element constitution of the ZIF-8/CDs complex. All the spectra were calibrated using C 1 s peak (284.8 eV). In Fig. 2G, the XPS survey spectrum of ZIF-8/CDs containing the four characteristic peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Zn 2p, was similar to that of ZIF-8. In the high-resolution XPS of C 1 s shown in Fig. 2H, its main characteristic peaks were deconvoluted into two peaks located at 284.62 eV and 285.41 eV, which were corresponded to the C-C bond and C-N bond in imidazole ring [28]. Meanwhile, two distinct peaks centered at 1021.32 eV and 1044.39 eV in the high-resolution XPS of Zn 2p were ascribed to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, implying that the Ⅱ valence state of Zn presented in ZIF-8/CDs complex [28]. The above-mentioned features implied that the chemical structure of ZIF-8 was preserved in the ZIF-8/CDs composite, in other words, the ratiometric-sensing platform integrated ZIF-8 with CDs through simply mixing was successfully established.

To elucidate the mechanism of TC detection using the as-proposed ZIF-8/CDs composite, the interaction between the ZIF-8/CDs composite and TC was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, when adding TC to the ZIF-8/CDs, a new fluorescence peak centered at 516 nm was formed in the ZIF-8/CDs/TC complex and the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 466 nm was almost constant compared with ZIF-8/CDs. It could be speculated that there was an interaction between TC and ZIF-8, leading to the fluorescence enhancement at 516 nm. To study the interaction between TC and ZIF-8, the optical absorption and fluorescence spectra of the reaction between TC and Zn2+, 2-methylimidazole, and ZIF-8 were performed in Fig. 3B and Fig. 3C, and it was found that Zn2+ and ZIF-8 could significantly enhance the fluorescence of TC centered at 516 nm. When TC was mixed with Zn2+, the absorption peak at 276 nm was blue-shifted, and the peak at 357 nm was red-shifted, which demonstrated the interaction of Zn2+ with TC. According to the previous report [29], these indicated that the reaction mechanism of ZIF-8 on TC detection might be related to the precursors of Zn2+. Fig. S3 also proved the enhanced intensity of TC in the range of 0–80 μ M in the presence of Zn2+, which was consistent with the previous reports. However, due to the special polyhedral structure of ZIF-8 with excellent porosity, which exhibited a superior response toward TC compared with Zn2+.

To further confirm the mechanism of TC detection using the ratio sensor ZIF-8/CDs complex, a series of characterization analyses and experimental data were conducted, including FT-IR, XPS, XRD, zeta potential, and so on. The FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8/CDs before and after the addition of TC were studied in Fig. 3D, after the addition of TC concentration, a broadband appeared in 1693–1539 cm−1, which were corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=N bond in ZIF-8 at 1585 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of C=O (1674 cm−1) and C=C (1614 cm−1) in TC, suggesting the interaction between ZIF-8 and TC [30]. As shown in Fig. 3E, there was no obvious change in the XPS survey spectra upon the addition of TC. We further investigated the contents of C and O elements before and after adding TC, the results implied that the proportion of C and O elements in the ZIF-8/CDs/TC increased (Table S1). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s, a new peak centered at 287.58 eV was associated with C=O linkage that derived from TC, proving the existence of TC (Fig. 3F). Two peaks at 1021.32 eV and 1044.39 eV corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 in the ZIF-8/CDs/TC were slightly offset compared with ZIF-8/CDs (Fig. 3G), probably due to the coordination of Zn2+ with TC. In Fig. 3H, the XRD patterns of the ZIF-8/CDs before and after TC treatment showed that the original characteristic peaks of the ZIF-8/CDs were also retained, indicating that TC did not lead to the collapse of the ZIF-8 framework structure and the crystal structure of ZIF-8 was not changed [27]. We further investigated the zeta potential data in Fig. 3I, the ζ potential of the ZIF-8/CDs complex was 12.2 mV, while the ζ potential of the ZIF-8/CDs/TC complex was 7.13 mV, indicating that ZIF-8/CDs and TC might tend to form aggregates through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and coordination interactions.

According to the previous reports, ZIF-8 not only absorbed TC but also restricted its conformational rotation, thereby reducing energy dissipation and enhancing fluorescence emission, meanwhile, the surface area of the porous ZIF-8 decreased remarkably after the addition of TC [31,32]. In order to prove that TC absorbed to the internal structure of ZIF-8 and emitted fluorescence concurrently, the aqueous solution of ZIF-8/CDs treated with TC should be dried and study the luminescence property under an ultraviolet lamp. Fig. S4 showed the fluorescence photographs of freeze-dried samples of TC, ZIF-8, ZIF-8/CDs, ZIF-8/CDs/TC under excitation of 365 nm UV lamp, in which the ZIF-8/CDs/TC power revealed yellow-green emitting appearance. It could be inferred that TC entered to the structure of ZIF-8 and emitted strong fluorescence. Considering all the above-mentioned situations, ZIF-8 could cause the AIE effect of TC, acting as the response signal, while blue-green emitting CDs were not sensitive to TC, using as the reference signal. Consequently, the ratiometric-sensing platform ZIF-8/CDs complex was established for the determination of TC, where the detection mechanism was considered to be an AIE effect when TC was absorbed into the framework of ZIF-8.

To achieve the best sensing performance of ZIF-8/CDs for TC detection, a series of experiments were conducted to optimize the experimental conditions. First of all, the effect of ZIF-8 concentration on the ratio intensity (F516/F466) was also studied when the concentration of CDs was kept constant. It was found from Fig. S5A that the ratio intensity F516/F466 gradually enhanced with the increase of ZIF-8 concentration and reached the highest at the ZIF-8 concentration of 15 mg/mL. Therefore, 15 mg/mL was selected as the best detection concentration. In addition, the effect of pH was investigated in the range of 5–10. As seen in Fig. S5B, the ratio intensity F516/F466 remained essentially constant in the pH range of 5–10. Then, pH=7 was selected to be the optimal condition. Moreover, the temporal kinetic behavior of the as-proposed sensor after the addition of TC was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5C, the maximum value of F516/F466 could be reached within 6 min and remained stable, thus 6 min was chosen as the best experimental condition. Finally, the influence of reaction temperature was also examined. It could be concluded from Fig. S5D that room temperature was chosen as the best reaction temperature.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the ZIF-8/CDs complex for sensing TC was examined. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 466 nm remained basically unchanged with the increase of TC concentration, while the fluorescence intensity of TC at 516 nm could be significantly enhanced through the AIE effect between ZIF-8 and TC [31]. In Fig. 4B, the CIE chromaticity diagram of the composite material ZIF-8/CDs after adding different concentrations of TC showed that the fluorescence color changed from Blue-green to light green, to pale yellow. As shown in Fig. 4C, there was an excellent linear relationship between the ratio intensity of F516/F466 and the added concentration of TC over the range from 0.03-60 μM. The linear regression equation was F516/F466 = 0.0217CTC+0.2984 (R2 = 0.9952) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 16.13 nM (calculated by the 3σ/k equation), which was relatively lower than the results reported in the previous studies (the details were described in Table S2). Compared with the strategy that used a single signal with ZIF-8 to detect TC (Fig. S6), the ratiometric fluorescence probe not only obtained a wider dynamic detection range, but also improved the detection sensitivity by more than 15 times, especially for the semi-quantitative detection using color conversion strategy in resource-limited area. Fig. 4D exhibited the reproducibility of ratiometric probe for the detection of TC. Due to this excellent repeatability, the ZIF-8/CDs composite could be potentially used to detect TC in actual samples.

For further validating the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the as-prepared ratiometric-sensing platform ZIF-8/CDs complex, a series of existing substances, including partial cations, anions, antibiotics, and amino acids, were investigated in the presence and absence of TC with the same experimental conditions. As described in Fig. S7, the F516/F466 value of the as-proposed ZIF-8/CDs complex could be increased significantly due to the addition of TC, while the other substances could not do that, even though the concentration of the substances was high. When TC coexisted with the other substance, the ratio intensity of the ZIF-8/CDs complex remained unchanged, suggesting that the ZIF-8/CDs complex had excellent specificity. Thus, the ratiometric-sensing platform ZIF-8/CDs complex was applicable to sensing TC in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real samples assay using fluorescence spectrophotometry
ce:section.ce:para: To further validate the practicability and feasibility of the as-proposed ratiometric sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of TC in real samples, the detection results using the as-proposed strategy were listed in Table 1 , where the recoveries of the spiked samples using the fluorescence spectrophotometry ranged from 92.30 % to 107.65 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) did not exceed 5.91 %, demonstrating that the as-proposed strategy could be utilized to sense TC in real samples with high accuracy and reliability. For comparison purpose, we further used the commercially available strategy, e.g., UV–vis spectrophotometry, to verify the determination of TC. The results were listed in Table S3, it could be concluded that the two methods received the results that were not of significant difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection using 3D-printing and MATLAB algorithm assisted RGB mode
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further apply the as-proposed strategy for the visual/on-site detection of TC in real samples with a semi-quantitative manner, particularly in resource-poor areas, it was crucial to establish a visual and instrument-free measurement with highly rapid and accurate. For this purpose, a novel ratiometric-sensing platform that combined 3D-printing technology and MATLAB algorithm was designed for on-site visualization detection of TC. The 3D-printing device consisting of four parts was shown in Fig. 5 A, which included a UV excitation light source, a sample slot, two filters (365 nm/400–640 nm) for filtering excitation, emission light and preventing the interferential source from entering the smartphone camera, as well as a dark chamber for connecting to a smartphone. Compared with the previous visualization device, the as-designed device was miniature and portable with the aid of 3D-printing technology. On the premise of providing convenience, the experiment variables are accurately controlled to achieve high accuracy and precision, which lays a solid foundation for the subsequent promotion of semi-quantitative visual analysis.

The detection process was shown in Fig. 5B, the image information (RGB values) protected by the designed 3D printing device was captured by the smartphone and uploaded to the designed mini program in MATLAB, subsequently a linear relationship between TC concentrations and the obtained RGB values was automatically established through computer language calculation, and then the corresponding TC concentration of the actual samples could be naturally read out (a video of detection process was presented in the supplementary materials) [14]. The detailed results of real samples were stated in Table S4, in which the recoveries of the spiked samples were 97.1 %-107.15 % and the RSD values did not exceed 6 %. Compared with the above-mentioned fluorometry and ultraviolet spectrophotometry, the results derived from the as-proposed strategy did not differ significantly, implying that the ZIF-8/CDs complex integrated 3D-printing technology and MATLAB algorithm could be used for visually on-site determination of TC in real samples with great feasibility and market prospect.

In summary, an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe ZIF-8/CDs complex by combining CDs with ZIF-8 through electrostatic interaction was developed to sense TC in real samples via the AIE mechanism with high specificity and sensitivity. Due to the polyhedral and porous structure of ZIF-8, TC could be absorbed to the inside of ZIF-8 and formed aggregates, and then boosted a greenish-yellow fluorescence at 516 nm through the AIE effect using as the response signal. Meanwhile, the blue-green-emitting fluorescence of CDs centered at 466 nm was insensitive to TC, acting as the reference signal. Accordingly, the ratiometric-sensing platform ZIF-8/CDs complex was successfully constructed for detecting TC, which not only boosted the detection sensitivity by more than 15 times compared with the measurement that utilized a single signal, but also received a wider dynamic detection range, especially for the semi-quantitative detection using color conversion strategy in resource-limited area. Hence, the as-proposed probe that combined 3D-printing technology and MATLAB algorithm was used to realize on-site semi-quantitative detection of TC in an economic, rapid, stable and reliable way without any sophisticated treatments and instruments. In this way, the afforded results using 3D-printing and MATLAB algorithm assisted RGB mode were not of remarkable difference compared with fluorescence spectrophotometry and ultraviolet spectrophotometry. Consequently, this research that integrates 3D-printing technology with MATLAB algorithm aims to develop a portable and intelligent ratiometric-sensing platform for on-site visual/accurate determination in a complicated environment/food matrix.

Ruimei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Dong Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Yuxin Guo: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jiawei Huang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation. Ruizhi Wen: Supervision. Hongchang Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ41064, 2022JJ90019, 2024JJ8279), and the Department of Education of Hunan Province (22B0245).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111730.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
